paragraphs.body: Craft Paper: Let the Characters Find the Plot

In the fostering of literary ambitions, my imagination has been nursed by two maternal figures, both vying to possess me, like the two women in Solomon’s court who claimed the child was theirs. On the one side, there is the nuanced sophistication of character driven literary-fiction. If I could invent a Raskolnikov who, in his decision making, revealed the innermost depths of the human psyche- I would doubtless be fulfilled. Yet, Dostoyefsky could never write about an alien or a car chase just because they are cool- and so he never wrote about aliens or car chases. Herein lies the appeal of the alternative, that of the absurd novelty found only in plot-driven genre fiction. Here, one can include all the “cool” things they might dream of, and connect the dots in as inelegant a way as necessary. The risk with such convoluted lines is a lack of clarity in the final picture. The judgement of Solomon was to cut the child in half, so each of the women could have their share (to which the true mother objects). I enjoy both styles of writing, but I find that when I try to appeal to both, I end up with a dead product. Through the course of this class, however, I have heard the character-driven mother’s objection.

annotations.comment: I bet he *would* have though — he was a pretty hip guy.

paragraphs.body: The first story I wrote for this class, “Emperor-Zilla: Hirohito’s Heir” demonstrated my initial interest in writing a character driven story, marred by my desire to include every idea I thought was cool, resulting in a confused story, where otherwise well defined characters do counterintuitive things as a means to get from point A to a predetermined point B. Initially, I had intended to write the story as a character study of a satirized version of Emperor Hirohito, depicting him as a idealistic and somewhat effeminate ruler who naively chooses to show mercy to a foreign threat (Godzilla-like monster), despite the recommendations of his general. In an effort to emasculate this character, I added the detail that he could not produce an heir. Somehow, this castration came to characterize the story, because my first draft concluded with Hirohito spiritually adopting the sea-monster, stating, “The heir to the throne must be powerful, but above all, he must value the lives of his countrymen. I think to my own childhood, when I dared not lay a finger on the other boys in our play fighting. Bring my son home to me immediately- he is truly like me both body and mind.” While I liked the idea of Hirohito adopting the sea-monster as his son and think that may have had its place in another story, I don’t think that it was honest to this version of Hirohito. This conclusion drastically shifts Hirohito’s character from naive to straight up delusional. In trying to include all of the elements I would have liked to fit in the plot, I rendered my story incomprehensible. Had I allowed Hirohito to make his own decisions as I had intended and stuck to the idea of a character-study, my story would likely have been more readable.

annotations.comment: Sure, but a first draft should be about trying a lot of different things to see what sticks.

paragraphs.body: Reading my classmates' stories has helped me understand that, if I am going to include bizarre elements into my fiction, I should limit the “clutter” so the foreign element can stand out more clearly. That is, especially in short fiction, it is usually best to stick to one idea and cling to it tightly. One example from recent memory is Avery’s “Living Stories”, which explored the idea of a fiction writer’s characters tormenting her in real life. This story stays on topic the entire time, and each sentence serves to prime the reader for the reveal. As such, the author-protagonist, Elinor Lawson, is characterized only as much as is necessary for the premise of the story. This prevents the reader from asking questions that aren’t relevant, therefore preventing the confusion that I think my story induced. Living Stories begins with the context, Elinor Lawson writes characters that “seem so real, readers often believe they can encounter them in the world outside the pages,” then supports this with fans at a book signing asking Elinor how she writes such realistic characters. Ultimately, when I reached the reveal of this story, that Elinor’s characters were somehow alive, I nodded my head, because it fit the groundwork that had already been laid out. This made for a satisfying reading experience.

annotations.comment: Hmm, that’s a great point. I think the story needs work (as all first drafts do) but I appreciate your point of cutting off readers’ questions.

paragraphs.body: While the “decluttering” of my stories might take a while, there is one thing that I began to actively work on to help in this process. In Bird by Bird, Anne Lamott suggests that “Plot grows out of character.” She describes how any attempt to force a plot onto a character will feel forced upon the reader as well. Instead, you should work to know your characters as well as possible so that they make the decisions for you, and it all reads organically. This means that lines drawn between plot points are much more elegant, because they are not drawn after the points, but rather they have become the points.

annotations.comment: This is probably her most astute observation in the book.

paragraphs.body: One story that especially reflects this principle is Caitlin Horrocks’ “Zolaria”. While dealing with an imaginative concept, a childhood fantasy, the magic of it comes from the deeply realized characters. Ultimately, the decisions of the narrator lead to the central conflict of the story- confronting her guilt of betraying a childhood friend who died young. This story does not rely on its “gimmick” to maintain interest. The narrator's friend, Hannah, is terminally ill and one day a girl at school snatches her wig and, “throws it to Carla Deleonn, who throws it to Mary-Alice, who throws it to Roberta, who throws it to me. And instead of giving it back to Hanna, I throw it to Andrea, who throws it to…” This decision felt realistic because it seemed like a chance for the narrator to fit in, to finally be cool, at the cost of her best friend. Ultimately, this decision weighs on the narrator, and as such, her strange behavior at the end of the story feels reasonable. In this way, this story feels like it unfolds organically, not forced.

annotations.comment: Yes, for sure. This story answers the question: what if unresolved guilt eats away at you for 15 years?

paragraphs.body: For my second story, “The Playground of Unspeakable Horror,” I sought to let the characters unfold in a more organic way, let them reveal themselves to me, so to speak. I had set out to write about a boy who was stuffed in a locker and gets visited by ghosts, but as I began to get a feel for the characters, I began to feel more and more as though the ghost parts of the story were tacked on and unnecessary. While I really wanted to write about ghosts, I felt that the characters were pulling me in another direction as I asked myself questions like, “What is motivating this character?” This question encouraged me to think of different possibilities for why someone might shove someone into a locker overnight, and why the victim would not be terribly bothered. Based on the behaviors of the characters which I had already written, the twist that the bully and his victim were lovers seemed to best fit the ending. Ironically, I don’t love this ending because it feels kind of cheap, but I think it makes more sense than the plot-driven idea I had in mind for the ghosts. The sacrifice of my personal wishes for the story nonetheless felt like a big moment of progress in my fiction writing.

annotations.comment: That’s good! You’re listening to the story.

annotations.context_left: me, so to speak. I had set out to write about a boy who was stuffed in a locker and gets visited by
annotations.context_right: , but as I began to get a feel for the characters, I began to feel more and more as though the ghost

paragraphs.body: One of the final stories I read for the class was “Rubberdust” by Sarah Thankam Mathews. This story covered many of the same themes that I intended to cover in my story, “The Playground of Unspeakable Horror,” and I feel that it showed me a way of covering these topics more gracefully. While the crimes of the bullies in “Rubberdust” were less severe than locking someone in a locker, the guilt of the perpetrator was much more severe than the bully in my story. The little girl feels so guilty of harassing her classmate that she asks her father to beat her, then “locks herself in the cupboard and pinches her legs all over.” In my story, none of the bullies experience much guilt. While Dave says he is sorry, his actions do little to reflect that meaningfully. Reading this story after writing “The Playground of Unspeakable Horror,” made me reconsider how realistic the behaviors of my characters were. It is one thing for a character to be consistent, it is another thing for the character to be believable.

annotations.comment: A good point, although the inclusion of the romance may make the bullied more sympathetic to the bully?

paragraphs.body: In conclusion, I think that over the course of this class, I have learned that rather than taking interesting ideas together, a better story is written by developing characters who make decisions that the reader will believe. Interesting ideas do not equate to a good story, and sometimes I have to sacrifice the ideas that I like to hold on to for the sake of a tighter narrative.

annotations.comment: Lovely paper, Everly. I’ve really enjoyed working with you this term and I do hope you will keep writing — you’re good at it!
